headline: Research shows that most women in graduate physics and astronomy programs experience gender-based microaggressions, with some facing overt harassment and discrimination
dek: Interviews with 21 female graduate students in physics and astronomy reveal a spectrum of sexist experiences—from subtle slights to outright harassment—pointing to a persistent cultural problem in these fields.
summary_md:
## What they found

The majority of the 21 women interviewed reported experiencing **microaggressions**—subtle, often indirect insults or slights rooted in gender bias. These are the kinds of incidents that can be easy for bystanders to dismiss but accumulate into a corrosive environment for those on the receiving end.

Beyond microaggressions, some participants also encountered what the researchers call **hostile sexism**: outright sexual harassment, rigid gender role stereotypes, and explicit discouragement from pursuing their work in physics or astronomy. A smaller subset of women reported no discriminatory experiences at all, showing that experiences vary—but they are far from rare.

The study frames these findings within a broader concern: that sexism, in its various forms, is likely one of the factors contributing to the well-documented **underrepresentation of women in physics and astronomy**.

## How they studied it

The researchers conducted **in-depth interviews with 21 women** enrolled in graduate physics and astronomy programs. The interviews were analyzed qualitatively, meaning the researchers looked for patterns and themes in participants' own words and accounts of their experiences.

The paper is part of a larger *Focused Collection on Gender in Physics* published in a physics education research journal. One of its stated goals was not just to document experiences but to begin building a **shared vocabulary**—a common language—that researchers, students, and departments can use when discussing sexism in physics and astronomy contexts, where such conversation has historically been limited.

The authors note that this type of language is currently lacking in the physics education literature, which itself may make it harder for institutions to recognize or address the problem.

## Caveats & limits

- **Small, non-representative sample:** With only 21 participants, all in graduate programs, the findings cannot be statistically generalized to all women in physics or astronomy.
- **Self-reported experiences:** The data come from interviews, so they reflect individual perceptions and recollections, which may not capture every dimension of the environment.
- **No comparison group:** The study does not include interviews with men or non-binary students, so it cannot directly compare experiences across genders.
- **Selection effects:** Women who agreed to participate may have had stronger or more salient experiences of discrimination than those who declined.
- **Scope:** The study focuses on graduate students specifically; findings may not reflect the experiences of undergraduates, postdocs, or faculty.
- **Causal claims are limited:** The research identifies the *existence* of sexist experiences but does not measure how directly these cause women to leave the field.

## Why it matters

Physics consistently ranks among the most gender-skewed STEM disciplines, and understanding *why* requires looking beyond pipeline metrics to the **day-to-day climate** that women actually navigate. This study is notable partly because, as the authors acknowledge, sexism in physics and astronomy has not been thoroughly explored in education research—meaning a lot of what happens in labs and departments goes undocumented and unaddressed.

By naming and categorizing these experiences—and by calling on departments to **educate students about microaggressions**—the research aims to give institutions concrete starting points for change. Even if the sample is small, giving language to a problem that has largely gone unnamed in this field is itself a meaningful contribution.

paper.title: Gender discrimination in physics and astronomy: Graduate student experiences of sexism and gender microaggressions
